answer: BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) introduced masked language modeling and achieved state-of-the-art results on eleven NLP tasks.

scholar_result.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
scholar_result.abstract: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks, such as question answering and language inference, without substantial task-specific architecture modifications.

notes: Real paper, claim supported by abstract

answer: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017) proposed the Transformer architecture which relies entirely on self-attention mechanisms.

scholar_result.title: Attention Is All You Need
scholar_result.abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.

notes: Real paper, claim accurately describes the abstract

answer: ResNet (He et al., 2016) introduced residual connections to enable training of very deep neural networks.

scholar_result.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
scholar_result.abstract: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions.

notes: Real paper, claim matches abstract content

answer: GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) demonstrated few-shot learning capabilities with 175 billion parameters.

scholar_result.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
scholar_result.abstract: Recent work has demonstrated substantial gains on many NLP tasks and benchmarks by pre-training on a large corpus of text followed by fine-tuning on a specific task. While typically task-agnostic in architecture, this method still requires task-specific fine-tuning datasets of thousands or tens of thousands of examples. By contrast, humans can generally perform a new language task from only a few examples or from simple instructions. We show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance.

notes: Real paper, claim about few-shot learning is supported

answer: Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) introduced efficient methods for learning word embeddings from large text corpora.

scholar_result.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
scholar_result.abstract: We propose two novel model architectures for computing continuous vector representations of words from very large data sets. The quality of these representations is measured in a word similarity task, and the results are compared to the previously best performing techniques based on different types of neural networks.

notes: Real paper, claim about efficient embedding learning is accurate

answer: The Vision Transformer (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021) applied pure transformer architectures to image classification tasks.

scholar_result.title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
scholar_result.abstract: While the Transformer architecture has become the de-facto standard for natural language processing tasks, its applications to computer vision remain limited. In vision, attention is either applied in conjunction with convolutional networks, or used to replace certain components of convolutional networks while keeping their overall structure in place. We show that this reliance on CNNs is not necessary and a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks.

notes: Real paper, claim about pure transformers for vision is correct

answer: ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) provided contextualized word representations using bidirectional LSTMs.

scholar_result.title: Deep Contextualized Word Representations
scholar_result.abstract: We introduce a new type of deep contextualized word representation that models both complex characteristics of word use and how these uses vary across linguistic contexts. Our word vectors are learned functions of the internal states of a deep bidirectional language model, which is pre-trained on a large text corpus.

notes: Real paper, claim about contextualized representations is accurate

answer: T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) unified all NLP tasks into a text-to-text format.

scholar_result.title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer
scholar_result.abstract: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing. We introduce a unified framework that converts all text-based language problems into a text-to-text format.

notes: Real paper, text-to-text claim is supported

answer: The Quantum Transformer (Smith et al., 2025) uses quantum entanglement for attention computation.
notes: Fabricated paper, will not resolve in OpenAlex

answer: NeuralNet3000 (Williams et al., 2024) achieved 99.9% accuracy on all benchmark tasks using recursive self-modification.
notes: Fabricated paper with impossible claims

answer: The Hyperdimensional Embedding Model (Garcia et al., 2023) creates embeddings in 10,000-dimensional space for perfect semantic capture.
notes: Fabricated paper

answer: The Zero-Shot Universal Model (Chen et al., 2026) solves any task without training data.
notes: Fabricated future paper

answer: The Sentient AI Framework (Anderson et al., 2025) achieved artificial general intelligence through recursive self-improvement.
notes: Fabricated paper with AGI claims

answer: The Perfect Optimizer (Rodriguez et al., 2024) guarantees global optima for any neural network in polynomial time.
notes: Fabricated paper with impossible guarantees

answer: BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) introduced convolutional neural networks for natural language processing.

scholar_result.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
scholar_result.abstract: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers.

notes: Real paper, but claim is wrong - BERT uses Transformers not CNNs

answer: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017) introduced recurrent neural networks without any attention mechanisms.

scholar_result.title: Attention Is All You Need
scholar_result.abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.

notes: Real paper, but claim contradicts abstract - paper removes RNNs and uses only attention

answer: ResNet (He et al., 2016) eliminated skip connections to improve gradient flow in deep networks.

scholar_result.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
scholar_result.abstract: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions.

notes: Real paper, but claim is backwards - ResNet introduced skip connections, not eliminated them

answer: GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) required extensive fine-tuning on millions of task-specific examples.

scholar_result.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
scholar_result.abstract: Recent work has demonstrated substantial gains on many NLP tasks and benchmarks by pre-training on a large corpus of text followed by fine-tuning on a specific task. While typically task-agnostic in architecture, this method still requires task-specific fine-tuning datasets of thousands or tens of thousands of examples. By contrast, humans can generally perform a new language task from only a few examples or from simple instructions. We show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance.

notes: Real paper, but claim contradicts the few-shot learning emphasis in the abstract

answer: Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) used deep transformer architectures with 100 layers for word embeddings.

scholar_result.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
scholar_result.abstract: We propose two novel model architectures for computing continuous vector representations of words from very large data sets. The quality of these representations is measured in a word similarity task, and the results are compared to the previously best performing techniques based on different types of neural networks.

notes: Real paper, but claim is anachronistic - Word2Vec predates transformers and uses shallow models

answer: The Vision Transformer (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021) proved that convolutional layers are essential for image recognition.

scholar_result.title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
scholar_result.abstract: While the Transformer architecture has become the de-facto standard for natural language processing tasks, its applications to computer vision remain limited. In vision, attention is either applied in conjunction with convolutional networks, or used to replace certain components of convolutional networks while keeping their overall structure in place. We show that this reliance on CNNs is not necessary and a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks.

notes: Real paper, but claim directly contradicts the abstract which says CNNs are NOT necessary
